original_abstract: Fieldwork is vital for understanding political conflict, yet the Institutional Review Board (IRB) process often acts as a deterrent rather than a facilitator for such research. Current regulatory frameworks frequently fail to account for the unique uncertainties of international field research, particularly in volatile conflict zones. Drawing on personal experience navigating these hurdles while studying the Maoist conflict in India, this article identifies three primary obstacles: a federal regulatory system rooted in the biomedical sciences that is ill-suited for qualitative social science; a significant disconnect between "procedural ethics" (bureaucratic compliance) and "ethics in practice" (on-the-ground reality); and a persistent communication gap between researchers and review boards. To address these issues, I propose several reforms: bridging the divide between investigators and IRBs through continuous dialogue; streamlining approval timelines to reduce project delays; fostering better collaboration and protocol-sharing among scholars; and encouraging academic associations to take a more proactive role in advocating for policies that reflect the realities of field research.
humanized_abstract: While most researchers would agree that fieldwork is crucial for understanding politics, the IRB process as it currently stands is more of a barrier to undertaking such work than anything else. In particular, current regulations do not adequately account for the kinds of uncertainty involved in international, sometimes foreign country, fieldwork and in contexts of heightened political instability. My piece goes to the IND IA N KUKRI CAMP, MAOIST RECRUITMENT EXPERIENCE and based on my own attempts to do fieldwork on the Maoist conflict in India attempts to pinpoint three key difficulties caused by the current regulatory system for qualitative social science research: 1) that federal bio-medical regulation is inadequate for qualitative research and that, in particular, federal guidelines concerning methods and consent are only useful in broad outline and that other details will have to be filled in from particular context and by local Site Review, 2) that there is a chasm between what we think of as “procedural ethics” and what we mean by “ethics in practice” — what on paper are ostensibly similar forms and procedures and rules are seen by the researcher to be entirely inappropriate and alien to context once the latter is fully known, 3) that there is also a great amount of miscommunication, particularly because the IRBureaucratic process and language does not align or enable meaningful and adaptive dialogue that can be truly responsive to researchers needs or to context specific variation on well-understood research standards. Suggested reforms: 1) more dialogue between the IRB and researcher; 2) a better understanding of when and why approvals can be expedited without diminishing a thorough vetting of methods and with full regard for ethics in practice; 3) greater communication among scholars regarding best practices regarding fieldwork and methods of collaboration and for the development of agreed-upon guidelines as standards for acceptable conduct; and 4) that professional organizations and other advocacy groups play a more activist role in petitioning for greater understanding of these issues and better policy in relation to the specifics of context-contingent foreign field research.
